title: Business Scenarios
kicker: A composable practice · plugs into the Essence kernel
lede: The TOGAF Business Scenario technique, re-expressed as a small, composable Essence practice. It adds almost no new structure — instead it becomes the engine that advances alphas you already have, sized the MVA way: one living document, not a wall of matrices.
text: Business Scenarios — an Essence Practice Extension Skip to main content Essence Way of Working Home Practices ▾ Four practices Business Scenarios Service Blueprint Min. Viable Architecture Min. Viable Governance Resources A composable practice · plugs into the Kernel + MVA/MVG deck Business Scenarios as an Essence practice The TOGAF Business Scenario technique, re-expressed as a small, composable Essence practice. It adds almost no new structure — instead it becomes the engine that advances alphas you already have, sized the MVA way: one living document, not a wall of matrices. TOGAF Business Scenarios Essence practice MVA-lean Customer area + front of the ADM loop Level · Enterprise / capability Where it plugs in The Customer area and the start of the ADM loop — inside Manage Concerns and Frame the Vision . What it adds One sub-alpha, six activities, one work product, twelve roles and four patterns — composed onto the kernel Customer spaces. What it advances Opportunity, Stakeholders, Requirements and Architectural Drivers — it's a way to get Drivers to Quantified . MVA discipline One Business Scenario document; SMART outcomes become measurable success criteria ; stop at fit-for-purpose. Show All Alphas Activities Work Product Roles Patterns ⎙ Print This practice is a synthesis: it expresses the Business Scenario method in Essence terms and maps it onto the existing Kernel + MVA/MVG deck. It paraphrases the technique rather than reproducing The Open Group guide — keep your licensed copy of the TOGAF® Series Guide: Business Scenarios as the authoritative source for the method itself. "Drives the alpha" notation shows which alpha state each element pushes toward. Sub-Alpha Customer Biz Scenario Business Scenario A sub-alpha of Architectural Drivers (and Requirements): the structured understanding of the business need, progressing through the method’s phases until it is good enough to drive the architecture. Premise Formed A simple premise, a capstone question and a candidate sponsor exist; a trigger is recognised. Verified A draft built from real stakeholders; the key questions have been tested face-to-face. Refined An agreed, fit-for-purpose statement of the need, with an agreed path forward. Released Placed in the repository as the high-level requirements that drive downstream work. Phases map to states →  Premise Formulation · Initial Verification · Refinement Alpha Customer Kernel Opportunity Why the change is worthwhile. Business Scenarios nails the problem, the “why”, and the value behind the effort. Identified The opportunity behind the premise is surfaced. Solution Needed The need for a solution is confirmed, not assumed. Value Established The value of solving it is understood and agreed. Business Scenarios drives it to →  Value Established · further by delivery & benefits practices Alpha Customer Kernel Stakeholders The people who affect or are affected. Engaging them early is the method’s single biggest source of value. Recognized Surfaced and identified as part of the premise. Represented Champions and representatives are named. Involved Participating in the workshops and readouts. In Agreement Agree the business need and the way forward. Business Scenarios drives it to →  In Agreement · further: Satisfied for Deployment / in Use Alpha Solution Kernel Requirements What the system must do. The released Business Scenario becomes the high-level requirements statement. Conceived The need for a new system is agreed. Bounded The purpose and theme are understood and bounded. Business Scenarios drives it to →  Bounded · carried to Fulfilled by requirements & delivery practices Alpha Solution MVA Architectural Drivers The significant concerns and quality attributes. The method’s SMART outcomes are exactly what make them measurable. Identified Concerns and their owners are surfaced. Prioritised The significant few are agreed and ranked. Quantified Each has a measurable success criterion (SMART). Business Scenarios drives it to →  Quantified · owned and carried on by the MVA practice Activity (parent) Biz Scenario ↳ Activity spaces: Explore Possibilities · Understand Stakeholder Needs · Understand the Requirements Run a Business Scenario Iteratively formulate, verify and refine the premise of the business need until it is fit to carry downstream — without over-iterating. Moves the alphas Business Scenario → Released · Opportunity → Value Established · Stakeholders → In Agreement · Drivers → Quantified Step ${i+1} / 5 Biz Scenario ↳ ${s[3]} ${s[0]} ${s[1]} Moves the alphas ${s[2]} Work Product Customer Biz Scenario Business Scenario (document) A single living document capturing the problem, environment, SMART outcomes, and human/computer actors — findings in the body, detail in appendices. The released version is the high-level requirements driver. Premise a one-page note: premise, capstone question, candidate sponsor. Draft verified with stakeholders; the five elements populated. Released refined, agreed, fit-for-purpose; in the repository. Evidences →  Opportunity · Stakeholders · Requirements · Architectural Drivers It feeds existing artifacts — it doesn’t multiply them The method can generate many matrices and catalogs. In an MVA the Business Scenario stays one document and its parts flow into work products you already keep: SMART outcomes / objectives feed → Architecturally-Significant Requirements Human & computer actors, roles feed → Architecture Definition (the MVA) Principles & constraints surfaced feed → Architecture Principles Released business need feed → Architecture Vision · Architecture Repository Role Customer Biz Scenario ${r[0]} ${r[1]} ${r[2].map(c=> Roles, not headcount The method deliberately separates roles from people — one person commonly plays several (a consultant who also analyses and authors). Configuring who plays what is part of the Plan step. Two roles overlap other pract

sections.h: A composable practice · plugs into the Kernel + MVA/MVG deck
It feeds existing artifacts — it doesn’t multiply them

title: An Essence Way of Working — Introduction
lede: This is the front door to a complete way of working built on the OMG / SEMAT
text: An Essence Way of Working — Introduction Skip to main content Essence Way of Working Home Practices ▾ Four practices Business Scenarios Service Blueprint Min. Viable Architecture Min. Viable Governance Resources Start here · the composed way of working A Way of Working, composed with Essence This is the front door to a complete way of working built on the OMG / SEMAT Essence standard: a kernel of universal things to track, plus four composable practices that move them. This page explains Essence, then shows how the kernel and practices fit together — and the logical order in which they move the alphas. OMG / SEMAT Essence 1 kernel + 4 practices Customer · Solution · Endeavor progress = alpha states Jump to What is Essence The language The kernel Levels of scale The practices Way of working Moving the alphas ⎙ Print Essence is an Open Group / OMG standard developed by the SEMAT community (Ivar Jacobson and others). This way of working composes the Essence kernel with four practices — Business Scenarios and Service Blueprint (discovery & service design), Minimum Viable Architecture and Minimum Viable Governance — synthesising ideas from TOGAF, the TOGAF Business Scenarios method, service blueprinting (Shostack, 1984) and Use-Case 2.0. The composition and card form are a synthesis for learning; keep your licensed standards as the authority. ${head('01','What is Essence?')} Essence is an OMG standard, created by the SEMAT initiative, that captures the common ground of every software endeavour as a thinking framework rather than a fixed method. Instead of following one methodology end to end, you compose your own way of working from small, reusable practices — and you judge progress by the health of a handful of essential things called alphas , not by the documents you have produced. A small language Six element types are enough to describe any way of working. A kernel The essential things every endeavour shares — always present underneath. Practices Composable units that plug into the kernel to form a full method. ${head('02','The language — six element types')} Everything in Essence — kernel and practices alike — is built from these six element types. The colours are used consistently across every card deck in this set. ${head('03','Progress is a state change')} Every alpha moves through ordered states , each with a checklist. Progress is an alpha reaching its next state — not a document being written. Laying the alpha cards on the table and agreeing their current state is how a team sees where it really is. For example, the Architecture alpha: Envisioned → Outlined → Demonstrated → Usable → Established → Evolving A practice's job is to move specific alphas from one state to the next. The whole way of working is just the practices, applied in a sensible order, until every alpha has reached the state you need. ${head('04','Three areas of concern')} The alphas are organised into three areas — the customer you serve, the solution you build, and the endeavour that does the work. ${head('05','The kernel')} The kernel is the universal scaffolding every endeavour shares: 7 alphas, 15 activity spaces and 6 competencies . On its own it moves nothing — it tells you what to track and that work must be done, never how . The "how" comes from practices composed on top. Foundation The Essence Kernel 7 alphas (incl. the generalised System ), 15 activity spaces, 6 competencies with the five-level ladder. Provides →  the alphas every practice advances Open the kernel deck → ${head('06','Levels of scale — naming the System')} Before you apply any of this, answer one question: what is the System? Essence's System alpha is the system of interest — and it is fractal. An enterprise is a system of systems; a solution is a sub-system of the enterprise; a configured product is a sub-system of the solution. The same kernel and practices instantiate at any level — and what is a "System" at one level is a "sub-system" at the level above. The boundary you draw decides what every alpha actually measures. Outer level Enterprise — System = the enterprise The enterprise is the system of interest. Enterprise architecture, the paved road, enterprise drivers and standing governance are owned here. Each solution below is a sub-system that rolls up into the enterprise's health. Middle level Solution / Project — System = the solution One endeavour delivering one solution. It inherits the enterprise architecture, paved road and governance as context (drivers and a way of working), and owns the architecture and decisions for this solution. Inner level Component — System = a configured product A single configured COTS product or service — a sub-system of the solution, tracked in its own right when it warrants it. So the parent–child relationships shift with the level. The clearest example: at enterprise scope, each solution becomes a sub-alpha of the System; at solution scope, the enterprise architecture and paved road are inherited context, not parts of your System. If System = the solution (project level) System means The one solution this endeavour delivers. Architecture / Decisions Owned — the architecture of this solution. Paved Road · Governance Inherited from the enterprise as context and guardrails. Drivers This solution's drivers, refined from enterprise drivers. If System = the enterprise System means The whole estate — a system of systems. Architecture / Decisions Enterprise architecture describing the estate. Paved Road · Governance Owned, enterprise-wide capabilities. Solutions Sub-alphas (sub-systems) that roll up into the enterprise. What flexes, and what doesn't Architectural Drivers and Architecture Decisions are forces and reasoning — they shape whatever System you box, so they stay first-class at every level. Architecture is the one that flexes: read as "the structural description of the system" it is an aspect (and could be a sub-alpha at that level); read as "the discipline of archi

sections.h: Start here · the composed way of working

title: The Essence Kernel
lede: The universal scaffolding every software endeavour shares: seven Alphas, fifteen Activity Spaces and six Competencies, across three areas of concern. Practices — Business Scenarios, MVA, MVG and your delivery practices — plug in on top. The kernel itself stays constant.
text: The Essence Kernel Skip to main content Essence Way of Working Home Practices ▾ Four practices Business Scenarios Service Blueprint Min. Viable Architecture Min. Viable Governance Resources The foundation · OMG / SEMAT Essence The Essence Kernel The universal scaffolding every software endeavour shares: seven Alphas, fifteen Activity Spaces and six Competencies, across three areas of concern. Practices — Business Scenarios, MVA, MVG and your delivery practices — plug in on top. The kernel itself stays constant. 3 areas of concern 7 Alphas 15 Activity Spaces 6 Competencies Show All Alphas Activity Spaces Competencies ⎙ Print This is the kernel only — it tells you what to track and that work must be done, not how . The “how” comes from practices composed on top. Kernel content follows the OMG Essence specification; descriptions are paraphrased to fit the card. Alpha ${AREA[a.area]} ${a.title} ${a.desc} ${states} Activity Space ${AREA[a.area]} ${a.title} ${a.desc} Level 1 Assists Understands the basics; follows guidance. Level 2 Applies Applies the competency in simple contexts. Level 3 Masters Works independently in complex situations; guides others. Level 4 Adapts Judges when and how to tailor the approach. Level 5 Innovates A recognised expert who extends the practice. Competency ${AREA[c.area]} ${c.title} ${c.desc} The kernel is not a method On its own the kernel moves nothing. To advance the Alphas through their states you compose practices on top: Business Scenarios for the customer front end, MVA to shape the solution, MVG to govern it, and delivery/engineering practices to build, run and realise value. The circumstances that make a new or changed system worthwhile. A commercial, social or business opportunity has been identified. The need for a software-based solution is confirmed. The value of a successful solution is established. It is clear a viable solution can be produced. A solution that addresses the opportunity has been produced. Operational use is creating tangible benefit. The people and groups that affect or are affected by the system. Stakeholders have been identified. Representatives are appointed and a way to work with them agreed. Representatives participate and meet their responsibilities. Representatives agree on the minimum needed to proceed. Satisfied for Deployment They agree the system is ready to deploy. Use of the system meets their expectations. What the system must do to address the opportunity and satisfy the stakeholders. The need for a new system is agreed. The purpose and theme are understood and bounded. The requirements form a coherent, consistent description. They describe a solution acceptable to stakeholders. Enough is addressed for the system to be acceptable. They are fully satisfied by the system. The system that delivers value — software, configured products, hardware, data and the services around them. A generalisation (extension) of Essence's Software System. Architecture Selected Architecture chosen to meet key requirements and risks. An executable version shows the key characteristics. The system is usable and shows the required quality. Tested and accepted for operational use. In use in an operational environment. No longer supported. The activity and effort applied to produce the result. The work has been requested. Preconditions for starting are in place. The work is proceeding. Tasks and risks are under control; going well. The work to produce results is complete. Everything wrapped up; lessons captured. The group actively developing, maintaining and supporting the system. Mission defined and conditions for growth in place. Enough members recruited to begin. Working as one cohesive unit. Working effectively and efficiently. Responsibilities discharged; no longer accountable. The tailored set of practices and tools the team uses to guide its work. Principles Established Principles and constraints agreed. Foundation Established Key practices and tools agreed. Practices and tools are in use. All members are using the way of working. Naturally adapted by the team and working well. Explore Possibilities Explore the opportunity, the stakeholders and the constraints to understand what is worth doing. Understand Stakeholder Needs Engage stakeholders to understand their real needs and reach agreement. Ensure Stakeholder Satisfaction Share results and gain stakeholder acceptance that the system meets their needs. Use the system in its operational context to generate benefit. Understand the Requirements Establish a shared understanding of what the system must do. Shape the system so it is easy to build, change, test and maintain. Implement the System Build a quality system that meets the requirements. Verify the system meets requirements and demonstrate its quality. Take the tested system and make it available for use. Support and sustain the system in live operation. Prepare to do the Work Set up the team and the work so the endeavour can succeed. Direct, prioritise and coordinate the work that is done. Help the team to function, improve and grow. Measure and assess progress and the health of the endeavour. Shut down the endeavour and close out the work cleanly. Stakeholder Representation Gather, communicate and balance stakeholders' needs and represent their views and decisions. Understand opportunities and needs and turn them into a shared, agreed understanding. Design and build an effective system. Test the system, verifying it meets requirements and demonstrating its quality. Inspire and motivate people to do what is needed and make good decisions. Coordinate, plan and track the work of the endeavour.

sections.h: The kernel is not a method

title: Essence — Training
lede: A training in the kernel, the four practices and the assessment workbook — how to use them together to move the alphas.
text: Essence — Training Skip to main content Essence Way of Working Home Practices ▾ Four practices Business Scenarios Service Blueprint Min. Viable Architecture Min. Viable Governance Resources Essence training Essence a practical way of working A training in the kernel, the four practices and the assessment workbook — how to use them together to move the alphas. Use → ← arrows to navigate · F11 for fullscreen Welcome · Agenda What we'll cover today Six sections — from the standard, through the practices, to the workbook that measures progress. 01 Foundations What Essence is, the language of element types, the kernel and the alpha state mechanic. 02 Levels of scale The System is the system of interest — and it is fractal. Pick the level before anything else. 03 The four practices Business Scenarios, Service Blueprint, MVA and MVG — what each does and the alphas it moves. 04 Composing the way Stacking the practices on the kernel and running them in a sensible order — including a decision guide. 05 The assessment workbook Measuring progress by alpha state — how to use the dashboard and tabs in practice. 06 Wrap-up Key takeaways and pointers to the full reference decks. Section 01 Foundations The Essence standard, in a handful of ideas. Foundations What is Essence — and why use it? What it is An OMG standard from the SEMAT initiative (Ivar Jacobson and others) that captures the common ground of any software endeavour as a thinking framework , not a fixed method. You get a small language , a kernel of universals every endeavour shares, and practices that plug in to form a complete method. Why use it Methodology fatigue is real. Big-process projects often produce big documents and small progress. Essence flips this: progress is judged by the health of essential things — alphas — not by the documents you wrote. It is lightweight, composable, and travels with you across teams and projects. The core move: compose your way of working from practices, and judge progress by alpha state. Foundations · Element types Six element types — the language Everything in Essence — kernel and practices — is built from these six. Colours are consistent across every deck in this set. Alpha Things you progress An essential thing whose health you track — it has ordered states . Activity Space What must be done A space for work the endeavour cannot skip; concrete activities fill it. Competency Abilities people bring Held at five levels: Assists → Applies → Masters → Adapts → Innovates. Work Product Tangible artifacts Evidence of an alpha's progress — a document, model, repo, decision record. Role Patterns of who does the work Hats people wear, not job titles. One person can wear several. Pattern Reusable rules of thumb Biases that shape every decision a practice makes. Foundations · Areas of concern Three areas of concern Every alpha lives in one of these — the customer you serve, the solution you build, the endeavour that does the work. Customer · Opportunity · Stakeholders Solution · Requirements · System · Architecture · Architecture Decisions · Architectural Drivers Endeavor · Work · Team · Way of Working · Governance Foundations · The kernel The kernel — universal scaffolding 7 alphas, 15 activity spaces, 6 competencies. On its own the kernel moves nothing — it tells you what to track, never how . The "how" comes from practices composed on top. Customer Opportunity The circumstances making a new or changed system worthwhile. Customer Stakeholders The people who affect or are affected by the system. Solution Requirements What the system must do to address the opportunity. Solution System The system that delivers the value — software, products, services. Endeavor Work The activity and effort applied to produce the result. Endeavor Team The group developing, maintaining and supporting the system. Endeavor Way of Working The tailored set of practices and tools the team uses. Plus + practice alphas Each practice you add brings its own alphas (e.g. Architecture, Governance). Foundations · The state mechanic Progress is a state change An alpha reaches its next state — that is progress, not the document you wrote. Every alpha moves through ordered states; each state has a checklist. Example · Architecture alpha 1 Envisioned → 2 Outlined → 3 Demonstrated → 4 Usable → 5 Established → 6 Evolving A practice's job is to move specific alphas from one state to the next. The whole way of working is just the practices applied in a sensible order until every alpha is where you need it. Foundations · The state checklist A state's checklist settles the dispute When a team disagrees on where it is, the checklist is the referee. The state is reached when every item is genuinely true. Architecture → Demonstrated ✔ The riskiest parts are proven via spike, reference or increment. ✔ The key decisions are validated by evidence. Stakeholders → In Agreement ✔ The minimal expectations of the stakeholder groups are agreed. ✔ The representatives are happy with their involvement. ○ The team's input is valued by the representatives. ○ Priorities are clear and perspectives balanced. The current state is the highest state where all items (and all earlier states) are checked. Section 02 Levels of scale Before you apply anything, answer one question: what is the System? Levels · Name the System The System is the system of interest — and it is fractal An enterprise is a system of systems; a solution is a sub-system of the enterprise; a configured product is a sub-system of the solution. The same kernel and practices instantiate at any level. Outer level Enterprise — System = the enterprise Enterprise architecture, the paved road, drivers and standing governance are owned here. Middle level Solution / Project — System = the solution Inherits enterprise paved road, drivers and governance as context; owns this solution's architecture and decisions. Inner level Component — System = a configured product A sub-system of the solution, tracked in its own

title: Essence Way of Working — Home
lede: One kernel of universal things to track, four composable practices that move them. Start with the introduction, explore the kernel, then work through each practice in the logical order below.
text: Essence Way of Working — Home Skip to main content Essence Way of Working Home Practices ▾ Four practices Business Scenarios Service Blueprint Min. Viable Architecture Min. Viable Governance Resources Essence Way of Working · Home A complete way of working, composed with Essence One kernel of universal things to track, four composable practices that move them. Start with the introduction, explore the kernel, then work through each practice in the logical order below. OMG / SEMAT Essence 1 kernel + 4 practices Customer · Solution · Endeavor progress = alpha states Jump to Start here Practices How they compose Workbook 01 Start here New to Essence? Read the introduction first, then skim the kernel reference to understand what the alphas are and how state progressions work. Introduction An Essence Way of Working The front door — what Essence is, the six element types, the kernel, how practices compose, and the logical order in which they move the alphas. Covers →  kernel + all four practices Open introduction → Foundation The Essence Kernel 7 alphas, 15 activity spaces, 6 competencies — the universal scaffolding every software endeavour shares, across three areas of concern. Provides →  the alphas every practice advances Open the kernel deck → Training Essence — Training A full training in the kernel, the four practices, and the assessment workbook — how to use them together to move the alphas. Format →  slide-style walkthrough Open training → 02 The four practices Each practice is a composable unit that plugs into the kernel and advances specific alphas. Run them in the order below — they form a loop that nests at every level of scale. Practice 1 · Discovery Business Scenarios The TOGAF Business Scenario technique re-expressed as a composable Essence practice. Discover and quantify the need before anything is committed. Advances →  Opportunity · Stakeholders Open practice → Practice 2 · Service design Service Blueprint Visualise the end-to-end service from both the customer's and provider's perspective — frontstage, backstage, and support processes. Advances →  Requirements · System Open practice → Practice 3 · Architecture Minimum Viable Architecture Architect the minimum, just in time, to retire significant risks and guide delivery. Decisions over documents — shapes the Solution alpha. Advances →  Architecture · System Open practice → Practice 4 · Governance Minimum Viable Governance Govern outcomes and guardrails, not gates. Decide at the last responsible moment, as close to the work as possible. Advances →  Endeavor · Way of Working Open practice → 03 How they compose The four practices stack on top of the kernel. Run them in a logical order — it is a loop, and it nests at every level of scale from enterprise to team. Practice 1 Business Scenarios Discover & quantify the need Practice 2 Service Blueprint Design the end-to-end service Practice 3 Min. Viable Architecture Architect just enough, just in time Practice 4 Min. Viable Governance Guard outcomes, not gates Foundation The Essence Kernel 7 alphas · 15 activity spaces · 6 competencies — always underneath 04 Assessment workbook EA Alpha Assessment Track alpha states across all seven kernel alphas. Sixteen tabs in three groups — tick the checklists and the dashboard moves. Documents lie; alpha states are honest. ⬇ Download workbook (.xlsx) Essence is an OMG standard developed by the SEMAT community (Ivar Jacobson and others). This way of working composes the kernel with four practices synthesising ideas from TOGAF, service blueprinting (Shostack, 1984) and Use-Case 2.0. Keep your licensed standards as the authority.

title: Minimum Viable Architecture
kicker: A composable practice · plugs into the Essence kernel
lede: Architect the minimum, just in time, to retire the significant risks and guide delivery — decisions over documents. MVA shapes the Solution; it pairs with Business Scenarios (which feeds it) and MVG (which governs it).
text: Minimum Viable Architecture — an Essence Practice Skip to main content Essence Way of Working Home Practices ▾ Four practices Business Scenarios Service Blueprint Min. Viable Architecture Min. Viable Governance Resources A composable practice · plugs into the Essence kernel Minimum Viable Architecture as an Essence practice Architect the minimum, just in time, to retire the significant risks and guide delivery — decisions over documents. MVA shapes the Solution; it pairs with Business Scenarios (which feeds it) and MVG (which governs it). TOGAF ADM B–F · H MVA Solution area pairs with MVG + Business Scenarios Level · The level of its System Where it plugs in The Solution area — shaping the system. Spans ADM phases B–F and H. What it adds 3 alphas (+1 sub-alpha) · 7 activities · 7 work products · 4 roles · 5 patterns · +1 competency (Architecture). What it advances Its own three alphas, and drives kernel System to Architecture Selected → Demonstrable . Depends on Business Scenarios for quantified drivers · MVG for guardrails · a delivery practice to build. Show All Alphas Activities Work Products Roles Patterns ⎙ Print MVA is one practice in a composed way of working. It owns the architecture-shaping elements split out from the former MVA/MVG bundle; governance now lives in the separate MVG practice. The TOGAF ADM mapping is a synthesis, not an Open Group standard. Alpha Solution MVA ${a.title} ${a.desc} ${st} ${a.foot} Sub-Alpha Solution MVA Paved Road The reusable foundation teams build on — the service chassis of approved products, patterns, reference setups and standards that makes the compliant choice the easy one. Conduct Architecture Review paves it and polices the exceptions. Seeded The need for a paved road is recognised; a first approved default or two exists. Established A coherent service chassis of approved products, patterns and standards is published and self-serviceable. Adopted Teams build on it by default; on-road choices need no review. Pervasive It is the norm across the estate; going off-road is the rare, justified exception. Evolving Continuously widened and pruned from review learnings and new drivers. Sub-alpha of Architecture · driven to → Evolving · MVG governs its adoption Alpha Solution Kernel System The system that delivers value — software, configured products, hardware and data. MVA selects its architecture and proves the riskiest parts before delivery builds it out. Architecture Selected Architecture chosen to meet the key drivers and risks. Demonstrable An executable version shows the key characteristics. Owned by Kernel · MVA drives it to → Demonstrable · carried to Operational by a delivery practice Adapting MVA for a COTS / buy-and-implement context When the company buys and configures commercial products rather than writing code, the alphas don't change — but how you advance them does. Conformance shifts from automated tests to a paved road of approved choices plus architecture review of the exceptions. Architecture Decisions become → product-selection & integration choices (decision matrix) Architecture Definition becomes → integration architecture, boundaries & standards products must meet Prove the Risky Parts becomes → proof-of-concept, vendor evaluation or sandbox pilot Significant Requirements become → selection & conformance criteria in the RFP Conformance assured by → paved roads + architecture review of deviations + SLA/telemetry monitoring Activity ${i+1} / ${acts.length} MVA ${a[0]} ${a[1]} Moves the alphas ${a[2]} Work Product MVA ${p[0]} ${p[2]} ${lv} Evidences →  ${p[1]} Role Solution MVA ${r[0]} ${r[1]} ${r[2].map(c=> Pattern MVA · ${p[0]} MVA ${p[1]} ${p[2]} How MVA composes with the rest Quantified business drivers in from → Business Scenarios practice Guardrails, principles, decision rights in from → MVG practice Architecture Selected → built & run handed to → delivery / engineering practice (missing) Review outcomes · Paved Road adoption out to → MVG (assurance & conformance) The significant concerns, goals, constraints and quality attributes that shape the architecture — the few things worth governing. Concerns and owners surfaced; candidate drivers listed. Significant drivers agreed and ranked; the trivial many deferred. Each significant driver has a measurable success criterion. Drivers reflected in decisions and the architecture. Drivers monitored and re-validated as context changes. The minimum set of structures, building blocks and views needed to satisfy the significant drivers and enable delivery — no more. Target direction, scope and value agreed on a page. Minimum viable structure, boundaries and interfaces defined. The riskiest parts proven via spike, reference or increment. Actively guiding and constraining delivery. In operation, delivering the intended outcomes. Changing under managed, continuous adaptation. Architecture Decisions The significant, costly-to-reverse choices that are the architecture — the unit of progress in an MVA. A significant decision point is recognised and owned. Options, trade-offs and the last responsible moment understood. Choice made; rationale and consequences recorded (ADR). Known and accepted by those it affects. Proven by evidence and outcome — or revisited. Capture and quantify the architecturally-significant drivers; agree their measurable success criteria. Drivers → Identified → Prioritised → Quantified Conduct Architecture Review Pave the road — publish approved products, patterns and standards as a self-serve service chassis — and review the off-road deviations and significant decisions against the drivers and principles. Paved Road → Established → Adopted · Decisions → Validated Envision the Architecture Agree the target and its value on a page; bound the scope. Architecture → Envisioned Shape the Minimum Architecture Define only the building blocks needed now across domains; frame and make the significant decisions. Architecture → Outlined ·

sections.h: A composable practice · plugs into the Essence kernel
Adapting MVA for a COTS / buy-and-implement context
How MVA composes with the rest

title: Minimum Viable Governance
kicker: A composable practice · plugs into the Essence kernel
lede: Govern outcomes and guardrails, not gates. Decide at the last responsible moment, as close to the work as possible, and assure lightly by evidence. MVG keeps the MVA practice honest without slowing delivery.
text: Minimum Viable Governance — an Essence Practice Skip to main content Essence Way of Working Home Practices ▾ Four practices Business Scenarios Service Blueprint Min. Viable Architecture Min. Viable Governance Resources A composable practice · plugs into the Essence kernel Minimum Viable Governance as an Essence practice Govern outcomes and guardrails, not gates. Decide at the last responsible moment, as close to the work as possible, and assure lightly by evidence. MVG keeps the MVA practice honest without slowing delivery. TOGAF Architecture Governance MVG Endeavor area pairs with MVA Level · Spans levels Where it plugs in The Endeavor area — it governs both the architecture and the way of working. What it adds 1 alpha · 5 activities · 5 work products · 2 roles · 5 patterns · +1 competency (Governance). What it advances The Governance alpha, and helps kernel Way of Working to Foundation Established . Depends on MVA for the decisions, drivers and architecture reviews it governs and assures. Show All Alphas Activities Work Products Roles Patterns ⎙ Print MVG is the governance practice split out from the former MVA/MVG bundle; the architecture-shaping elements now live in the separate MVA practice. The TOGAF mapping is a synthesis, not an Open Group standard. Alpha Endeavor MVG Governance The lightweight decision rights, guardrails and assurance that keep delivery aligned with intent without slowing it down. Initiated Decision rights and minimal guardrails agreed — who decides what. Engaged A lean cadence operates; teams know the guardrails. Guiding Decisions flow within guardrails; only exceptions escalate. Assured Conformance is evidenced where it genuinely matters. Effective Governance demonstrably enables delivery and is continuously tuned. Owned by MVG · driven to → Effective Alpha Endeavor Kernel Way of Working The tailored practices and tools the team uses. MVG sets the decision rights and guardrails that form its governance foundation. Principles Established Governance principles and constraints are agreed. Foundation Established Decision rights, guardrails and cadence are in place. Owned by Kernel · MVG drives it to → Foundation Established · carried on by delivery & improvement practices Activity ${i+1} / 5 MVG ${a[0]} ${a[1]} Moves the alpha ${a[2]} Work Product MVG ${p[0]} ${p[2]} ${lv} Evidences →  ${p[1]} Role Endeavor MVG ${r[0]} ${r[1]} ${r[2].map(c=> Pattern MVG · ${p[0]} MVG ${p[1]} ${p[2]} How MVG composes with the rest Decisions, drivers, building blocks governed for → MVA practice Review outcomes · Paved Road adoption in from → MVA · assured as conformance Principles & guardrails out to → MVA & delivery teams Lean cadence & decision rights shapes → kernel Way of Working Agree decision rights and minimal guardrails; ratify a small set of principles; set up the governed repository. Governance → Initiated · Way of Working → Foundation Established Stand up a lean rhythm and publish the guardrails so every team knows them. Governance → Engaged Let decisions flow within the guardrails; escalate only the exceptions; unblock the teams. Governance → Guiding Gather architecture-review outcomes and paved-road conformance signals where conformance genuinely matters. Governance → Assured Continuously remove or lighten any governance that has stopped earning its keep. Governance → Effective A short charter: scope, intended outcomes, decision rights and a definition of done. Decision rights & DoD agreed Architecture Principles A small set (≈7) of guardrails, each with rationale and implications — the backbone of MVG. Agreed, owned, visible to all Architecture Working Agreement A lean contract between architecture and delivery: guardrails, paved roads and how to escalate. Shared understanding Lightweight agreement teams cite Conformance Evidence Architecture-review outcomes and paved-road signals plus light review notes — evidence that matters, gathered continuously, not gates. Review outcomes visible Exceptions noted & resolved Architecture Repository (governed) Governance · Architecture The single, minimal, accessible home for the artifacts — curated and pruned so it stays trustworthy. Current, owned, pruned Provides mandate and funding; sets and protects the decision rights that make MVG work. A small, fast, principle-based body (a lean Architecture Board or guild). Assures outcomes, not paperwork. Govern outcomes and guardrails, not gates Set clear boundaries, let teams move freely inside them, and inspect results — not compliance theatre. Decide at the last responsible moment Push decisions as close to the work and as late as is responsible, where the most information exists. Make the right thing the easy thing Provide paved roads, templates and defaults so the compliant path is also the fastest path. Assure by evidence, lightly Base conformance on architecture-review outcomes and paved-road signals, sized to risk. No risk, no ceremony. Govern just enough, just in time Add governance only where its absence would do real harm — and remove it when it stops earning its keep.

title: Resources
lede: All workbooks, reference materials, and page links in one place.
text: Resources — Essence Way of Working Skip to main content Essence Way of Working Home Practices ▾ Four practices Business Scenarios Service Blueprint Min. Viable Architecture Min. Viable Governance Resources Resources · Essence Way of Working Downloads & references All workbooks, reference materials, and page links in one place. 01 Downloads Printable or workable files to use alongside the way of working. 📊 Excel workbook EA Alpha Assessment Track alpha states across all seven kernel alphas. Sixteen tabs in three groups — tick the checklists and the dashboard moves. Documents lie; alpha states are honest. ⬇ Download 02 All pages Direct links to every page in this site — useful for bookmarking or opening for print. Home Essence Way of Working Introduction An Essence Way of Working Foundation The Essence Kernel Training Essence — Training Practice 1 · Discovery Business Scenarios Practice 2 · Service design Service Blueprint Practice 3 · Architecture Min. Viable Architecture Practice 4 · Governance Min. Viable Governance Essence is an OMG standard developed by the SEMAT community (Ivar Jacobson and others). This way of working composes the kernel with four practices synthesising ideas from TOGAF, service blueprinting (Shostack, 1984) and Use-Case 2.0. Keep your licensed standards as the authority.

title: Service Blueprint
kicker: A composable practice · plugs into the Essence kernel
lede: Visualise the service delivery process from both the customer's and the provider's perspective — customer actions, frontstage and backstage, support processes and physical evidence — across the lines of interaction, visibility and internal interaction. Like a use case, the blueprint is sliced and progressed, and it drives the same alphas.
text: Service Blueprint — an Essence Practice Skip to main content Essence Way of Working Home Practices ▾ Four practices Business Scenarios Service Blueprint Min. Viable Architecture Min. Viable Governance Resources A composable practice · plugs into the Essence kernel Service Blueprint as an Essence practice Visualise the service delivery process from both the customer's and the provider's perspective — customer actions, frontstage and backstage, support processes and physical evidence — across the lines of interaction, visibility and internal interaction. Like a use case, the blueprint is sliced and progressed, and it drives the same alphas. Service Design · Shostack 1984 Service Blueprint Customer → Solution pairs with Business Scenarios & MVA Level · Solution / service Where it plugs in The Customer area , reaching into Solution — the front of the loop, alongside Business Scenarios. What it adds 1 sub-alpha · 5 activities · 2 work products · 6 roles · 5 patterns · +1 competency (Service Design). What it advances Like a use case: Requirements → Acceptable, Stakeholders → In Agreement, Opportunity → Value Established, and it specifies the System . Depends on Business Scenarios for the need; MVA & delivery to build the supporting systems. Show All Alphas Activities Work Products Roles Patterns ⎙ Print Service blueprinting was introduced by G. Lynn Shostack (“Designing Services That Deliver”, Harvard Business Review, 1984); the layered/line structure follows common service-design practice (e.g. Nielsen Norman Group). Expressing it as an Essence practice — and the use-case analogy — is a synthesis, not an official standard. It owns one sub-alpha and advances kernel alphas the way a Use-Case 2.0 use case does. Sub-Alpha Customer Svc Blueprint Service Blueprint A map of one service journey across all its layers — customer actions, frontstage, backstage, support processes and physical evidence — split by the lines of interaction, visibility and internal interaction. Sliced and progressed like a use case. Scoped The service journey and customer to blueprint are chosen and bounded. Frontstage Mapped The customer-actions row (the foundation) and frontstage touchpoints are laid out across the line of interaction. Blueprinted Backstage, support processes/systems and physical evidence added — complete across all three lines. Validated Walked through with customers and staff; fail points, waits and hand-offs surfaced and agreed. In Operation The service is delivered to the blueprint and the blueprint is kept live as it evolves. Sub-alpha of Requirements · driven to → In Operation Alpha Solution Kernel Requirements What the service must do across every touchpoint. The blueprint expresses it — exactly as a use case expresses functional requirements. Conceived The need for the service is agreed. Bounded Purpose and scope of the journey are bounded. Coherent The layers form one coherent, consistent description. Acceptable Describes a service acceptable to customers and staff. Owned by Kernel · drives it to → Acceptable · carried to Fulfilled by delivery Alpha Customer Kernel Stakeholders Customers and the people who deliver the service — frontstage and backstage staff are actors in the blueprint. Recognized Customer and the delivering roles are identified. Represented Representatives for each lane are named. Involved They contribute to and walk the blueprint. In Agreement They agree the service and the improvements. Owned by Kernel · drives it to → In Agreement Alpha Customer Kernel Opportunity The value the service creates. Blueprinting makes the value — and where it leaks — visible. Identified The service opportunity is surfaced. Solution Needed A service-based solution is confirmed. Value Established The value of delivering it well is agreed. Owned by Kernel · drives it to → Value Established Alpha Solution Kernel System The supporting systems and processes below the line of internal interaction. The blueprint specifies what they must do — like a use case identifying what the system must support. Architecture Selected The supporting systems and processes the service needs are identified and specified. Operational Once the blueprinted service runs, its supporting systems are in operational use. Owned by Kernel · blueprint specifies the supporting systems · built by MVA & delivery Modeled like a use case A service blueprint behaves like a Use-Case 2.0 use case — itself an Essence practice from Ivar Jacobson, an Essence co-author. It is a sub-alpha you slice and progress, and it drives Requirements, Stakeholders, Opportunity and the System the same way a use case does. The difference: a use case stops at the system boundary, while a blueprint spans the whole service — frontstage, backstage and the supporting systems. A single journey (or a slice of one) is the natural unit to take from Scoped to In Operation. One blueprint built top-down through the layers, in ${acts.length} activities. Each names the kernel activity space it fills and the alpha states it reaches. Activity ${i+1} / ${acts.length} Svc Blueprint ↳ ${a[3]} ${a[0]} ${a[1]} Moves the alphas ${a[2]} Work Product Svc Blueprint ${p[0]} ${p[2]} ${lv} Evidences →  ${p[1]} Role Customer Svc Blueprint ${r[0]} ${r[1]} ${r[2].map(c=> Roles, not headcount A small team commonly combines these — a designer who also facilitates and authors. Two overlap other practices: the Service Owner is typically the same person as the Business Scenarios Sponsor / MVG Architecture Sponsor, and the Support & Systems Owner is the bridge into the MVA practice. Customers and frontstage/backstage staff are kernel Stakeholders playing blueprint roles. Pattern SB · ${p[0]} Svc Blueprint ${p[1]} ${p[2]} ${p[3]? How Service Blueprint composes with the rest Customer-journey map (the customer's view) in from → journey mapping · a companion, not part of this practice Quantified need & drivers in from → Business Scenarios practice Servic
